ontology.foodex2_description: Burrito — wheat flour tortilla filled with meat, rice, beans, and vegetables
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pulled pork burrito as a composite dish; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared mixed dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard pulled pork burrito weighing approximately 300 g, consisting of a large flour tortilla filled with seasoned pulled pork, steamed rice, black beans, shredded cheese, lettuce, tomato, and cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Pulled Pork Burrito — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F05020097)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05020097 for Pulled Pork Burrito. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are preserved verbatim. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Burrito with beef and beans' (NDB 21340) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this item; value adapted proportionally. Micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed burrito / flour tortilla with beans and rice composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pulled pork burrito. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for a mixed burrito with flour tortilla, beans, and rice (GI ~50–55 range). Flour tortilla GI ~30 (low), white rice GI ~72 (high), black beans GI ~30 (low); composite weighted estimate yields GI ~52 (low). Glycemic load calculated as (52 × 17.17 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 26.8 per full serving; per 100 g GL = (52 × 17.17) / 100 = 8.9; per serving (300 g) GL = 26.8, rounded to 15.3 per half-serving reference of 150 g for standard reporting. GL per serving (300 g full burrito) = 26.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 507 mg per 100 g — substantially exceeds the 400 mg per 100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit or avoid pulled pork burritos due to the high sodium content (507 mg/100 g) contributed by seasoned pork, cheese, and tortilla; if consumed, request reduced seasoning on the pork, omit cheese, and limit portion to half a burrito to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (17.2 g/100 g) with low GI (~52) partially mitigated by dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g) and protein (11.9 g/100 g) slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pulled pork burrito has a low estimated GI (~52) and moderate carbohydrate load, making it conditionally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion is controlled to one half-burrito (~150 g, ~26 g carbohydrate); substituting white rice with brown rice or extra beans further lowers the glycaemic response, and avoiding added sour cream reduces saturated fat that can worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (507 mg/100 g) and estimated saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) from pork and cheese raise cardiovascular risk, partially offset by dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g) from beans and vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of high sodium and saturated fat in a pulled pork burrito warrants caution; limit to a half-portion, request low-sodium seasoning, omit cheese, and add extra beans and vegetables to increase fibre and potassium intake that support blood pressure and lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pulled pork is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); sodium 507 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit pulled pork burritos due to the high arachidonic acid content of pork that directly worsens joint inflammation, the elevated sodium that promotes tissue oedema, and the presence of tomato (nightshade) in the filling that may trigger individual RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A pulled pork burrito is a Mexican-American composite dish consisting of a large wheat flour tortilla wrapped around a filling of slow-cooked, seasoned pulled pork, steamed white rice, black beans, shredded cheddar cheese, and mixed fresh vegetables including lettuce, tomato, and cabbage. The pork is typically braised or slow-roasted until tender and then shredded, seasoned with spices such as cumin, chilli powder, garlic, and smoked paprika. The tortilla is soft and pliable, providing a neutral starchy wrapper. The overall flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced, and hearty, with contrasting textures from the tender pork, creamy beans, fluffy rice, and crisp vegetables. Per 100 g, the dish provides 204 kcal, 11.9 g protein, 9.7 g fat, and 17.2 g carbohydrate with 2.8 g dietary fibre and 507 mg sodium. The high sodium content (507 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension management without modification. The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 52 (low) reflects the moderating effect of beans and protein on the carbohydrate response. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and moderate in saturated fat, posing cardiovascular and hypertension risk at full portions, while the fibre and protein content provide some metabolic benefit for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets without modification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a pulled pork burrito?
Is pulled pork burrito good for diabetes?
Can I eat a burrito if I have high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in a pulled pork burrito?
Is a pork burrito high in protein?
What is the GI of a burrito?
Is pulled pork burrito suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05020097 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (burrito with meat and beans). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database composite methodology. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for this dish type.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.20 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Burrito with beef and beans' (NDB 21340), adapted proportionally for pork; HPB does not report saturated fat for this record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an exact pulled pork burrito match as of 2026. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for pulled pork burrito was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input record incorrectly listed dietary_tags as 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' — these have been corrected to false as the dish contains pulled pork and cheese. 5. The input occasion_tags listed 'breakfast' — this has been reclassified to lunch/dinner/takeaway as a burrito is not a typical breakfast item. 6. The input glycemic_index was set to 0 with confidence 0.316 from an 'Embedded GI Database' (a forbidden source); this has been replaced with an approved analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology. 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_00002473 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as defensible anchors for burrito as a composite wheat-wrapped meat and bean dish; these are the closest available ontology matches.
